title: Fine-Tuning, PEFT & Quantization
description: LoRA/QLoRA, full vs PEFT, quantization (GGUF, Q6_K), serving trade-offs.

cards.front: LoRA vs. full fine-tuning — when do you use each?
cards.back: Full fine-tuning updates all weights — most powerful but very expensive (optimizer state, gradients, activations for every parameter). LoRA freezes the base and trains tiny injected adapter matrices (<1% of params) — cheap, fast, tiny swappable files. Use LoRA/PEFT almost always; full fine-tuning only to change fundamental behavior with abundant data/GPUs.
distractors: Full fine-tuning freezes the base model and trains adapters; LoRA retrains every weight from scratch.
LoRA trains all weights but in lower precision, while full fine-tuning uses FP32 throughout.
Use full fine-tuning for small data and LoRA only when you have data-center-scale GPUs.
LoRA and full fine-tuning are identical in cost; the only difference is the file format.
LoRA quantizes the model to 4-bit, whereas full fine-tuning keeps it at 16-bit.
Full fine-tuning produces a tiny adapter file; LoRA produces a full-size checkpoint per task.
cards.quizAnswer: Full fine-tuning updates all weights (expensive); LoRA freezes the base and trains tiny adapters — cheap and usually preferred.

cards.front: How does LoRA work? What are rank and alpha?
cards.back: The fine-tune's weight change is approximately low-rank, so LoRA learns ΔW = B·A with a tiny rank r (4-64) instead of a full d×d update; effective weight is W + (α/r)·B·A with W frozen. Rank r = adapter capacity (higher = more expressive, more overfit risk); alpha α scales the adapter's contribution (heuristic α = 2r).
distractors: LoRA trains the full weight matrix but caps the learning rate at α and the epochs at r.
Rank is the number of GPUs used and alpha is the batch size; both are infrastructure settings.
LoRA adds random noise of magnitude α to r% of the weights to regularize the model.
Rank sets the quantization bit-width and alpha sets the temperature during inference.
ΔW is approximated as A + B (a sum of two large matrices), where r is their shared dimension.
Alpha is the dropout rate applied to the adapters and rank is the context length.
cards.quizAnswer: LoRA learns a low-rank update B·A with small rank r; alpha scales its contribution (effective α/r), the base stays frozen.

cards.back: LoRA on top of a 4-bit quantized frozen base model — you only need accurate gradients for the small adapters, so you quantize the frozen base (~4x memory savings) and train adapters in higher precision. Lets you fine-tune a large model on a single consumer GPU. Key parts: NF4 data type, double quantization, paged optimizers.
distractors: Quantizing the LoRA adapters to 4-bit while keeping the base model in full FP16 precision.
Training the full model in 4-bit, including all base weights, with no frozen layers.
A queueing system (Q = queue) that schedules many LoRA fine-tunes across GPUs.
Running LoRA inference on quantized hardware; it has nothing to do with training.
Distilling a large model into a 4-bit student model using LoRA as the loss function.
Quantization-aware training that simulates 4-bit math during a full fine-tune.
cards.quizAnswer: LoRA on a 4-bit quantized frozen base, so you can fine-tune large models on a single consumer GPU.

cards.front: What is quantization and what's the trade-off?
cards.back: Reducing the numerical precision of weights (and sometimes activations) — e.g. FP16 down to 8-bit or 4-bit ints; a 7B model goes from ~14 GB to ~4 GB. Trade-off: smaller and faster (less memory bandwidth) vs. some accuracy loss. PTQ quantizes an already-trained model (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF); QAT simulates quantization during training (better, costlier).
distractors: Reducing the number of model layers to make it smaller, at the cost of reasoning depth.
Compressing the training dataset into fewer examples so fine-tuning runs faster.
Converting weights to higher precision (FP32) to improve accuracy at the cost of memory.
Pruning attention heads that contribute least to the output, measured by gradient magnitude.
Caching frequent activations so repeated tokens skip computation entirely.
Splitting the model across multiple GPUs so each holds a lower-precision shard.
cards.quizAnswer: Lowering weight precision (e.g. FP16 to 4-bit) shrinks and speeds the model, trading a little accuracy for much less memory.

cards.front: GGUF, GPTQ, AWQ, bitsandbytes — when each?
cards.back: GGUF (llama.cpp format): CPU and mixed CPU/GPU inference on consumer hardware, many K-quant bit-widths — go-to for local/on-prem. GPTQ: GPU PTQ using second-order info, fast 4-bit GPU inference. AWQ: protects salient weights, often slightly better than GPTQ at 4-bit for GPU serving. bitsandbytes: easy on-the-fly 8/4-bit, great for QLoRA training.
distractors: GGUF is for GPU-only data-center serving; bitsandbytes is the fastest production inference format.
GPTQ and AWQ are CPU formats, while GGUF only runs on TPUs.
bitsandbytes is a quantization-aware training method that retrains the model in 2-bit.
AWQ averages the weights across layers; GPTQ is a tokenizer, not a quantizer.
GGUF is a fine-tuning library and GPTQ is a vector database format.
All four are identical formats with different names used by different vendors.
cards.quizAnswer: GGUF/llama.cpp for local/CPU, GPTQ and AWQ for GPU production serving, bitsandbytes for QLoRA training.

cards.front: What does 'Q6_K' mean and what breaks at lower bit-widths?
cards.back: In GGUF naming, the number is average bits per weight and _K is the K-quant scheme (block-wise scale/min, mixed precision). Q6_K ≈ 6 bits, near-lossless; Q4_K_M ≈ 4-bit, the quality/size sweet spot; Q8_0 ≈ full quality. Q6/Q5 ≈ FP16; Q4 shows minor degradation; below 4 bits (Q3/Q2) coherence, reasoning and arithmetic fall off a cliff.
distractors: Q6_K means the model has 6 billion parameters; the K denotes a Korean-trained variant.
The number is the number of layers quantized, and lower numbers mean higher quality.
Q6_K is 6-bit but lower bit-widths are lossless because K-quant fully recovers precision.
Quality improves as bit-width drops because smaller weights generalize better.
The number is the context window in thousands of tokens; Q2 means a 2k window.
Below 4 bits nothing breaks; the model simply runs slower on the same hardware.
cards.quizAnswer: GGUF naming: ~6 bits per weight with the K-quant scheme; near-lossless. Below 4-bit, reasoning and coherence break down.

cards.front: How do you build a fine-tuning dataset? What about data quality?
cards.back: Quality and diversity over quantity — a few thousand clean, representative examples beat a noisy huge set (PEFT overfits fast on junk). Format to match the task in the model's chat template (special tokens matter), cover diverse phrasings/edge cases, hold out a validation set to catch overfitting, and filter/verify any synthetic data rather than blindly trusting the generator.
distractors: Quantity over quality — collect millions of examples and let scale wash out the noise.
Skip the validation set; with PEFT the model cannot overfit because the base is frozen.
Use raw web text with no formatting, since chat templates only matter at inference.
Trust synthetic data fully — a stronger model's output never needs filtering.
Make the dataset as narrow as possible so the model specializes sharply.
Maximize duplicate examples so the most important cases dominate training.
cards.quizAnswer: Quality and diversity over quantity: clean, well-formatted, diverse examples, a validation set, and verified synthetic data.

cards.front: What is catastrophic forgetting and how does PEFT help?
cards.back: When you fine-tune hard on a narrow task, the model can overwrite general capabilities — good at your task, worse at everything else (full fine-tuning is most prone). PEFT/LoRA mitigates it structurally: base weights are frozen, so you add a small adapter rather than overwriting the foundation, largely preserving general knowledge.
distractors: It's when the GPU runs out of memory mid-training and loses the checkpoint; PEFT uses less memory so it can't happen.
It's the model forgetting the current conversation; PEFT helps by enlarging the context window.
It's overfitting to the validation set; PEFT prevents it by training on more epochs.
PEFT makes forgetting worse because adapters overwrite the attention layers directly.
It's the loss of quantization precision over time; PEFT re-quantizes the weights to fix it.
It's catastrophic gradient explosion; PEFT clips gradients to a fixed norm to stop it.
cards.quizAnswer: Fine-tuning narrowly can overwrite general skills; PEFT freezes the base and adds adapters, so general knowledge is preserved.

cards.front: What is Chain-of-Thought, and is it training or prompting?
cards.back: Getting the model to produce intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer, which improves multi-step tasks (math, logic, planning). Primarily a prompting technique ('think step by step' or few-shot worked examples), but you can also fine-tune on reasoning traces to bake it in. Related: self-consistency (sample paths, majority vote) and ReAct (reasoning + tool actions).
distractors: A quantization scheme that chains low-bit blocks together to recover precision.
A retrieval method that follows links between documents to build a reasoning chain.
Strictly a training-only technique; it cannot be triggered through prompting.
A decoding strategy that always takes the longest possible output sequence.
An attention variant that lets tokens attend only to the previous token in a chain.
A way to chain multiple models together so each handles one reasoning step in sequence.
cards.quizAnswer: Producing intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer; mainly a prompting technique, but it can be fine-tuned in.

cards.front: RAG vs. fine-tuning vs. prompting — the decision framework?
cards.back: 1) Start with prompting (incl. few-shot) — cheapest, exhaust it first. 2) Need external/changing knowledge or provenance → RAG. 3) Need consistent behavior, format, tone, or a missing skill → fine-tune (LoRA first). 4) They compose (fine-tune for how + RAG for what). 5) Need computation, live data, or actions → tools/agents. Common mistake: 'fine-tune on our docs' is usually a RAG problem.
distractors: Always fine-tune first; prompting and RAG are only fallbacks when fine-tuning fails.
Use RAG to change the model's tone and fine-tuning to inject fresh facts.
Never combine approaches — pick exactly one of prompting, RAG, or fine-tuning per app.
For computation and live data, fine-tune the model on the data rather than using tools.
Prompting is the most expensive option, so reserve it for when RAG and fine-tuning are too costly.
Fine-tuning is the right default for injecting changing knowledge that must be cited.
cards.quizAnswer: Prompt first, RAG for knowledge, fine-tune for behavior; they compose, and use tools for computation or actions.

cards.front: Have you done distributed / multi-node training? (How to answer honestly)
cards.back: Don't bluff: state plainly that your fine-tuning has been single-GPU PEFT/QLoRA, then show conceptual grasp — data parallelism (replicate, split batch, all-reduce gradients), tensor/model parallelism (split a layer across GPUs), pipeline parallelism (split layers), and frameworks like DeepSpeed ZeRO and FSDP — and redirect to genuine single-node depth.
distractors: Claim deep multi-node production experience to match the job description, then hope it doesn't come up.
Say you can't discuss training topics you haven't run, and change the subject entirely.
Insist multi-node and single-node training are the same, so the distinction doesn't matter.
Explain that data parallelism splits a single layer's matrices across GPUs.
State that DeepSpeed ZeRO replicates the full optimizer state on every GPU to be safe.
Pretend pipeline parallelism means running several independent training jobs at once.
cards.quizAnswer: Be honest: single-node PEFT/QLoRA only, but explain data/tensor/pipeline parallelism and ZeRO/FSDP, then redirect to real depth.
